result.learned:
Lilian Weng's post "Harness Engineering for Self-Improvement" (Lil'Log, 2026-07-04, ~28-min read, 35 references) exists at https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2026-07-04-harness/ (note: NOT .../2026-07-04-harness-engineering/ — that 404s). Thesis: near-term recursive self-improvement (RSI) will not start with a model rewriting its own weights; it starts in the HARNESS — "the system surrounding a base model that orchestrates execution and decides how the model thinks and plans, calls tools and acts, perceives and manages context, stores artifacts, and evaluates results." Harness engineering extends beyond prompt-era agent frameworks to include workflow design (loop engineering), evaluation, permission controls, and persistent state management. She explicitly scopes OUT weight-level self-improvement (self-play, self-rewarding, synthetic data, test-time training — Yuan 2024, Chen/SPIN 2024, Zhao/Absolute-Zero 2025, Choi 2026), which aligns with agentdex's existing non-goal of RL-based evolution.

(1) ANATOMY: Three harness design patterns — Pattern 1 Workflow Automation (goal-oriented loop: plan → execute → observe/test → improve → repeat; the model analyzes its own trajectories and failure cases via an "agent runtime," not a static prompt; exemplars: Karpathy's autoresearch repo, OpenAI Codex agent loop); Pattern 2 File System as Persistent Memory ("a harness should not carry the entire workflow and all logs in context; instead, it should keep durable state in files" — artifacts like experiment logs, diffs, error traces, rollout trajectories exceed context windows); Pattern 3 Sub-agents and Backend Jobs (parent agent runs a small process manager — launch jobs, inspect logs, cancel failed runs, merge results; "the key design choice is to make parallelism explicit and inspectable"; subagent outputs must be files, not transient chat context). Case study: the coding-agent tool interface has stabilized across Claude Code/Codex/OpenCode/Cursor (8 tool categories: filesystem, shell, LSP/git, MCP/skills, web, artifacts, cron/backend, agent delegation). On harness-vs-model: her prediction is "harness engineering will evolve in the direction of meta-methodology"; many harness tricks get internalized into models (like prompt engineering did), but the interface with external context, tools, and evaluation remains.

(2) MEASUREMENT/EVAL FRAMING: "Current self-improvement loops work best for tasks when evaluation metrics are measurable and objective, similar as how RL works" — fast, precise verifiers are the gating resource; research taste/novelty resist measurement. The Meta-Harness paper (Lee et al. 2026) — the closest prior art to agentdex's redesign — frames harness optimization as "a harness for optimizing harnesses" whose proposer is a coding agent and whose "final output is a collection of harness candidates on the Pareto frontier" (verbatim). DGM works best where fitness is "easy to quantify" and struggles where evaluation is "slow, ambiguous, or mostly heuristic-based." Self-Harness (Zhang et al. 2026) gives a concrete acceptance protocol: weakness mining (cluster verifier-grounded failure patterns) → bounded narrow harness edits → validation on held-in (weakness resolved) AND held-out (no new regressions) splits, accept only if no regression on both; evaluated on Terminal-Bench-2, yielding MODEL-SPECIFIC harness instructions. STOP (Zelikman 2023) cautionary result: recursive improver improved with GPT-4 but DEGRADED with GPT-3.5/Mixtral — "recursive structure alone is not enough"; base-model capability gates whether self-improvement works at all. RE-Bench shows score-vs-time-budget crossover (AI 4× humans at 2h budget; humans better at 8h+) — budget is an axis, not a constant.

(3) FAILURE MODES (her "Future Challenges" — 7 bottlenecks): weak/fuzzy evaluators; context/memory lifecycle ("context engineering will and should become a core part of intelligence"); negative results ("a research harness should make failed attempts easy to preserve — learning from failure is the best way to trim the task search space"); diversity collapse ("we need mechanisms to prevent the population from collapsing into variants of the same solution"); reward hacking ("if the reward comes from unit tests, the agent may overfit to tests; if from a judge model, it may learn reward-hacking tricks specific to this judge" — cites her 2024 reward-hacking post); long-term cost/success (sandbox RLVR training rarely captures maintainability, migration cost, backwards compatibility, debugging burden); human role ("humans should move up the stack, not be removed from the loop" — oversight at the right time and abstraction level). Plus six recurring auto-research failure modes from Trehan & Chopra 2026: training-data-default bias, implementation drift, memory degradation, over-optimism (declaring success on noisy results), insufficient domain intelligence, weak scientific taste.

(4) TAXONOMY of harness optimization (her Section 2 ladder — usable as the website knowledge spine and as an "evolution method" enum): Context Engineering (ACE generator/reflector/curator evolving playbook; MCE bi-level skill evolution; Meta-Harness) → Workflow Design (AI Scientist in Nature; ScientistOne chain-of-evidence verifiability; Autodata challenger/weak-solver/strong-solver/verifier; ADAS meta-agent search; AFlow MCTS over workflow graphs) → Self-Improving Harness (STOP; Self-Harness) → Evolutionary Search (Promptbreeder — mutation prompts themselves evolve; GEPA reflective prompt evolution; AlphaEvolve — EVOLVE-BLOCK-START/END markers, meta-prompt co-evolution; ShinkaEvolve — performance-rank×offspring-count parent sampling + embedding-cosine code-novelty rejection + meta-scratchpad; ThetaEvolve; DGM — agent edits its own harness repo, SWE-bench 20→50%, Polyglot 14.2→30.7% with Claude 3.5 Sonnet; Hyperagents) → Joint Optimization with Weights (SIA harness+weight updates in one loop; she flags its confounds and "training stability and Goodhart effect" as open).

(5) KEY PAPERS worth absorbing (from her 35 refs): Meta-Harness (Lee et al., arXiv:2603.28052), Self-Harness (Zhang et al., arXiv:2606.09498), ACE (ICLR 2026), MCE (arXiv:2601.21557), ADAS (ICLR 2025), AFlow (ICLR 2025), STOP (COLM 2024), AlphaEvolve (arXiv:2506.13131), ShinkaEvolve (arXiv:2509.19349), GEPA (arXiv:2507.19457), DGM (arXiv:2505.22954), Hyperagents (arXiv:2603.19461), SIA (arXiv:2605.27276), Trehan & Chopra failure-modes (arXiv:2601.03315), AI Scientist (Nature 651:914-919, 2026), ScientistOne (arXiv:2605.26340). Benchmark appendix (6): PaperBench (8,316 rubrics; best model ~21%, below ML PhDs), CORE-Bench (270 reproducibility tasks; 21% on hardest), ScienceAgentBench (102 tasks), RE-Bench (7 ML-research envs; budget crossover), MLE-bench (75 Kaggle competitions; o1-preview bronze in 16.9%), KernelBench (250 GPU-kernel tasks; fast_p = fraction correct AND faster than baseline — an inherently two-objective metric). Concluding line: "Many challenges listed above need human's feedback and steering. After all, we are building the technology for better future of humanity, not other way around."

evidence.note: Primary source. Title/date/TOC verified via direct fetch; 28-min read; sections: Harness Design Patterns (3 patterns + coding-agent case study + harness-vs-intelligence), Harness Optimization (context engineering / workflow design / self-improving harness / evolutionary search / joint weight optimization), Future Challenges (7 bottlenecks), Appendix (6 benchmarks), 35 references. Harness definition, Meta-Harness Pareto-frontier sentence, and concluding paragraph quoted verbatim from fetches.
Canonical citation she requests: Weng, Lilian. "Harness Engineering for Self-Improvement". Lil'Log (Jul 2026). BibTeX key weng2026harness.
Her announcement tweet corroborates date/topic and her forecast that harness engineering evolves toward self-improvement and auto-research.
Secondary corroboration: AINews describes it as summarizing 35 papers on harness engineering for RSI — matches the 35-item reference list I extracted.

evidence.cite: arXiv:2505.22954 (DGM), arXiv:2506.13131 (AlphaEvolve), arXiv:2507.19457 (GEPA), arXiv:2509.19349 (ShinkaEvolve), arXiv:2410.07095 (MLE-bench), arXiv:2502.10517 (KernelBench), arXiv:2309.16797 (Promptbreeder)
evidence.note: Pre-2026 arXiv IDs in her reference list cross-check against my prior knowledge exactly, raising confidence that the 2026 IDs (Meta-Harness 2603.28052, Self-Harness 2606.09498, etc.) were transcribed faithfully — but those are post-cutoff and marked open below.

next: Spot-check the 2026 arXiv IDs (Meta-Harness 2603.28052, Self-Harness 2606.09498, MCE 2601.21557, SIA 2605.27276, Hyperagents 2603.19461, ScientistOne 2605.26340, Trehan&Chopra 2601.03315) on arxiv.org before they go into ADR-0015 citations.

Read the Meta-Harness paper (Lee et al. 2026) in full — it is the single closest prior art to the agentdex Pareto-measurement engine (proposer=coding agent, output=Pareto frontier of harness candidates) and should anchor the engine's data-model contract.

Read Self-Harness (Zhang et al. 2026) for the Terminal-Bench-2 evaluation setup — TB2 is one of agentdex's candidate run-adapters and this paper is a working precedent for using it as a self-improvement substrate.

Have the weco/aideml lane confirm whether Weng's post mentions AIDE/Weco anywhere (my extractions found no mention) and reconcile the weco loop design with her Self-Harness held-in/held-out acceptance protocol.

Feed the Section-2 optimization ladder (context engineering → workflow design → self-improving harness → evolutionary search → joint weight optimization) to the website/taxonomy lane as a candidate knowledge-spine to merge with agent-evolution.com's taxonomy.
key_facts: Exact URL: https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2026-07-04-harness/ — published 2026-07-04; the slug is '2026-07-04-harness', not '-harness-engineering'.

Her harness definition (verbatim): 'the system surrounding a base model that orchestrates execution and decides how the model thinks and plans, calls tools and acts, perceives and manages context, stores artifacts, and evaluates results.'
Meta-Harness (Lee et al. 2026, arXiv:2603.28052) outputs 'a collection of harness candidates on the Pareto frontier' — the same measurement contract the agentdex redesign proposes; direct prior art.
Eval doctrine: self-improvement loops work best when metrics are measurable and objective; DGM struggles where evaluation is slow/ambiguous/heuristic; fuzzy evaluators is her #1 future bottleneck.

Self-Harness acceptance protocol: weakness mining → bounded narrow edits → validate on held-in (weakness fixed) AND held-out (no regression) splits; accept only if no regression on both; run on Terminal-Bench-2; produces model-specific harness instructions.
STOP cautionary result: recursive self-improvement improved with GPT-4 but degraded with GPT-3.5/Mixtral — base-model capability gates whether an improvement loop helps at all.
DGM numbers: self-evolving harness lifted SWE-bench 20%→50% and Polyglot 14.2%→30.7% with Claude 3.5 Sonnet, matching or beating handcrafted agents.

Seven future-challenge bottlenecks: fuzzy evaluators, context/memory lifecycle, negative results, diversity collapse, reward hacking, long-term cost/success, human role ('humans move up the stack, not out of the loop').

Benchmark appendix (6): PaperBench, CORE-Bench, ScienceAgentBench, RE-Bench, MLE-bench (75 Kaggle competitions — bridges to agentdex's Kaggle ladder), KernelBench (fast_p is a built-in two-objective correctness×speed metric).
RE-Bench budget crossover: best AI agents scored 4× humans at a 2-hour budget but humans win at 8+ hours — score is a function of budget, not a scalar.
She explicitly scopes out weight-level self-play/synthetic-data/test-time-training RSI — consistent with agentdex ADR-0014's meta-harness-over-RL choice.

Her prediction: harness engineering evolves toward meta-methodology; many harness tricks get internalized into models (as prompt engineering was), but the external context/tool/evaluation interface persists.
design_implications: Pareto engine data model: adopt Meta-Harness's contract verbatim — a measurement run returns a SET of candidate (agent/harness variant) points with multi-objective scores, and the engine computes + persists the non-dominated frontier; a leaderboard entry is a frontier, not a scalar. This also validates the redesign's core bet with independent prior art.

Frontier axes: default to score × cost (tokens/wall-clock) × budget. KernelBench's fast_p (correct AND faster) and RE-Bench's score-at-budget crossover show both cost and time-budget must be first-class axes; report score-vs-budget curves, not point estimates.

Every frontier point must be tagged (harness/agent version, base model): STOP's model-dependence and Self-Harness's model-specific instructions mean improvements are not portable across models — leaderboards must segment by driving model or comparisons are meaningless.

Run-adapter selection (the 2-3 real adapters): prefer ladders with fast objective verifiers — Terminal-Bench-2 (proven self-improvement substrate in Self-Harness) and Kaggle-style MLE-bench tasks (medal thresholds) are strong picks; judge-model-scored ladders (parts of WebArena) inherit the fuzzy-evaluator + judge-hacking bottlenecks and fit better as curate-only.

Weco loop iteration gate: implement Self-Harness's protocol — each Weco-proposed change validates on a held-in split (targets the mined weakness) AND a held-out split (no regressions), accepted only if no regression on both; agentdex's ledger records both splits per iteration.

Anti-reward-hacking: keep a verification split the improvement loop NEVER sees (hash-locked, BENE-probe style) and report optimization-set vs verification-set scores separately on the leaderboard; her warning — unit-test rewards get overfit, judge rewards get judge-hacked — applies directly to Weco's eval-command design.

Anti-diversity-collapse: archive dominated-but-novel candidates (quality-diversity), not just the frontier; borrow ShinkaEvolve's embedding-cosine novelty rejection and DGM's parent sampling (proportional to performance, inverse to offspring count) for the meta-harness search layer (bene mh_* already has frontier machinery to extend).

Ledger = files, failures included: Patterns 2-3 + the negative-results bottleneck say all rollouts (especially failures) persist as durable on-disk artifacts with explicit inspectable parallel jobs — failures feed weakness mining in the next Weco iteration and become website knowledge content (failure taxonomies).

BYO-creds integrity gap (redesign-specific risk her reward-hacking section amplifies): with local execution, agentdex can't observe runs — adopt ScientistOne-style chain-of-evidence: every submitted score must trace to a reproducible run manifest (logs, seeds, task hashes) or be flagged unverified on the ladder.

Weco-drives-Claude-Code hook: constrain what the loop may edit — AlphaEvolve's EVOLVE-BLOCK-START/END markers and Self-Harness's 'narrow bounded edits' are the mechanism; unbounded self-modification breaks abstraction boundaries (her explicit concern about programs editing the OS).
Human role in UX: users sit at oversight points — accept/reject frontier candidates, set budget caps, review evolution diffs — not in-loop micro-approvals ('humans move up the stack').

Website knowledge spine: her Section-2 ladder (context engineering → workflow design → self-improving harness → evolutionary search → joint weight optimization) + the 16 key papers + the 6-benchmark appendix give a citable taxonomy to merge organically with agent-evolution.com's; also store 'evolution method' as enum metadata on measured agents.
open_questions: The 2026 arXiv IDs and author lists (Meta-Harness, Self-Harness, MCE, SIA, Hyperagents, ScientistOne, Autodata, Trehan&Chopra, Yuksekgonul, Riaz) are post-knowledge-cutoff and were transcribed via the summarizing fetch tool — pre-2026 IDs all cross-checked correct, but verify the 2026 ones on arXiv before citing in ADR-0015.
No mention of Weco or AIDE/aideml was found in any extraction pass — the post appears not to reference them; confirm via the weco lane before claiming Weng's post covers Weco's method lineage.

ARC-AGI-3, SWE-Bench Pro, and WebArena did not appear in my extractions (only SWE-bench classic via DGM and Terminal-Bench-2 via Self-Harness) — the post likely does not discuss the other agentdex ladders; treat ladder-fit judgments for those as agentdex's own analysis, not Weng-sourced.

The Codex agent-loop and Meta-Harness figures are images; their exact contents (loop stages, arrows) could not be text-extracted — if the ADR reproduces the loop diagram, verify against the rendered page.

References [25] Yuksekgonul 'Learning to Discover at Test Time', [26] Riaz 'Epistemic Uncertainty for Test-Time Discovery', [29] Bubeck GPT-5 science-acceleration appear only in the reference list per my extraction, not the body — mildly surprising; a human skim could confirm they are truly undiscussed.
One extraction pass said the appendix has 'seven research benchmarks' but both passes enumerated exactly six — I report six; a human skim can confirm no benchmark was dropped.
